bio.intro: My strength is my end-to-end path: I started in {qa}, moved into {funcional}, which lets me approach {frontend} with a complete view of {quality}, {ux}, and business needs.

meta.title: Experience — Joan Manuel Romero
meta.description: Career path: Frontend Developer at Ministerio Público Fiscal, Functional Analyst at UXOR IT, and QA Analyst at Nahual IT.

bullets: Led the full migration of a legacy internal system (Django Admin) to a modern Vue 3 SPA, reducing load time by ~60% and removing UI dependencies on the backend.
Designed and maintained the reusable component library with PrimeVue and Tailwind CSS, adopted team-wide and cutting new-view development time by 40%.
Implemented route lazy loading, code splitting and Vite bundle optimizations, achieving an initial bundle under 200 KB gzipped.
Delivered in 2-week Scrum cycles, coordinating with UX, Backend and QA to ensure continuous delivery with zero regressions.
Gathered and documented over 80 user stories for client Victoria Seguros, ensuring full traceability from business requirements to technical implementation.
Cut misunderstandings between business and development by 35% through detailed flow diagrams and use cases validated with stakeholders before each sprint.
Facilitated grooming and refinement meetings with teams of up to 8 people, driving backlog estimation and prioritization.
Executed over 500 functional and regression test cases for high-volume clients including Mercado Libre, Merck and Sancor Seguros.
Built and maintained an automation suite with Robot Framework + Selenium covering 70% of critical flows, shrinking manual regression time from 2 days to 4 hours.
Championed accessibility testing (WCAG 2.1) for public sector and healthcare clients, becoming the team's technical reference in this area.

meta.title: Projects — Joan Manuel Romero
meta.description: Tech solutions with real impact: Goludos (football app & web), NutriAmor (AI nutrition), Digital Invitations and practice projects.

projects.subtitle: Tech solutions focused on real impact and scalability.

goludos-mobile.title: Goludos Mobile App
goludos-mobile.description: Complete mobile ecosystem for amateur and professional football. Live results, detailed stats and a fluid native UX.
goludos-mobile.impact: Solves information fragmentation in football leagues, centralizing data and delivering instant updates to thousands of active users.
goludos-mobile.learned: The hardest part was maintaining state consistency between the Ionic app and the Node.js backend under high concurrency on match days. I solved it by combining optimized polling with local caching, eliminating 95% of redundant requests.

caseStudy.problem: Amateur soccer league organizers managed everything through WhatsApp groups and scattered Excel spreadsheets. Loading results was manual, players couldn't see their stats in real time, and notifications arrived late or not at all.
caseStudy.solution: We built a native mobile app with Ionic and Angular that centralizes tournament management, fixtures, results, and statistics. The Node.js backend with PostgreSQL handles scoring and ranking logic automatically.
caseStudy.decisions: I chose Ionic over React Native because the team already had Angular experience, which reduced the learning curve and delivery time. PostgreSQL over Firebase due to the relational nature of tournament data (teams, matches, goals, assists). Push notifications use Capacitor's native FCM integration.
caseStudy.outcomes: The app was published on Google Play Store and has active users in Argentina. Result loading time dropped from hours (manual updates) to seconds. Organizers went from managing leagues across multiple tools to a single platform.

goludos-web.title: Goludos Web
goludos-web.description: Scalable web platform that complements the mobile experience. Robust admin panel and complex data visualization for league managers and fans.
goludos-web.impact: Lets organizations manage tournaments efficiently and stay accessible from any desktop device.
goludos-web.learned: The key learning was designing a granular role and permission system in Angular without coupling authorization logic to the component layer. I implemented composable route guards that kept presentation and access control completely separate.

caseStudy.problem: Administrators needed a complete management interface to operate leagues: create tournaments, load fixtures, validate results, and generate reports. The mobile app was only for players and referees.
caseStudy.solution: Web panel in Angular with Bootstrap for full system administration. It shares the same backend as the mobile app, so changes reflect in real time across both platforms.
caseStudy.decisions: Bootstrap over Tailwind because the panel was an internal project with tight deadlines and Bootstrap sped up prototyping of complex forms and tables. The shared backend architecture with mobile avoided duplicating business logic.
caseStudy.outcomes: Administrators can manage complete tournaments from the browser without manual coordination. The panel reduced the setup time for a new tournament from 2 hours to 20 minutes.

nutriamor.title: NutriAmor
nutriamor.description: AI-powered nutrition platform. Detailed diet tracking, automatic nutritional analysis of recipes and personalized health reports.
nutriamor.impact: Improves adherence to nutrition plans through immediate, personalized feedback — supporting nutritionists and empowering patients.
nutriamor.learned: Integrating an AI API with variable response times was the main challenge. I designed a queue system with progressive UI feedback so users never perceived the latency as a failure, which improved the retention rate for the AI analysis feature.

caseStudy.problem: Users of nutritional plans drop out in the first weeks because feedback on their progress only arrives days later during in-person appointments. Without immediate reinforcement, adherence drops drastically.
caseStudy.solution: Web platform integrating a computer vision API to analyze food photos and estimate macronutrients instantly. The system automatically compares actual intake vs. the nutritionist's plan and generates personalized alerts and suggestions.
caseStudy.decisions: Vue 3 with Composition API for the fine-grained reactivity needed by real-time progress dashboards. Firebase for auth and Firestore to persist meal history without own infrastructure. The food analysis AI is consumed via external API to avoid training costs.
caseStudy.outcomes: Actively in development. Internal tests show meal logging time reduced from 5 minutes (manual notes) to 30 seconds (photo + confirmation). Currently in beta with a group of nutritionists.

invitaciones-digitales.title: Digital Invitations
invitaciones-digitales.description: Full-stack SaaS platform for creating and sharing customizable digital invitations. Covers three modules: commercial landing page, visual invitation editor, and mobile-first final product shareable via WhatsApp.
invitaciones-digitales.impact: Replaces paper invites and WhatsApp groups with a centralized experience featuring real-time RSVP, countdown timer, photo gallery, and gift list — all accessible through a single link.
invitaciones-digitales.learned: Architecting a WYSIWYG-style editor with Vue 3 reactivity was the core challenge. I chose a Pinia store with normalized section structures and a reactive preview system, which made adding new editor sections possible without touching the render logic.
invitaciones-digitales.videoAlt: Demo of a completed quinceañera invitation

landing.title: Commercial Landing Page
landing.description: Conversion-focused product presentation site. Communicates the value proposition, showcases styles by event type (weddings, quinceañeras, birthdays), explains the 3-step process, and includes pricing, real client testimonials, and FAQ. Designed to capture leads via WhatsApp.

editor.title: Visual Invitation Editor
editor.description: Self-service dashboard where clients enable and configure each section of their invitation: cover, countdown, event info, photo gallery, playlist, map, gift list, and RSVP confirmation. Changes reflected in real time with an integrated mobile preview.

invitation.title: Finished Invitation
invitation.description: The product guests receive: a mobile-first web experience shareable via WhatsApp as a unique link. Features animations, background music, live countdown, interactive gallery, and one-tap RSVP confirmation.

gallery.f: Pricing table — Self-service and Custom plans
gallery.g: Visual editor with real-time invitation preview

caseStudy.problem: Social event invitations (weddings, quinceañeras, birthdays) were coordinated via WhatsApp images or PDFs with no interactivity. There was no way to confirm attendance, manage the guest list, or share updatable logistical information.
caseStudy.solution: SaaS platform where organizers create interactive invitations with a visual editor, unique link per event, RSVP attendance confirmation, countdown timer, and post-event photo gallery. All in a mobile-first experience.
caseStudy.decisions: Vue 3 with Vite for build speed and developer experience. Firebase Hosting + Firestore for serverless without infrastructure: each invitation is a real-time document, RSVPs update instantly. Tailwind for the visual editor where style iteration speed was critical. The demo video is served directly from hosting to minimize mobile latency.
caseStudy.outcomes: Platform in production with real clients. Invitations eliminate WhatsApp screenshot sharing and centralize all event logistics. Creating a complete invitation takes under 10 minutes.

meta.title: TIL · Joan Romero
meta.description: Small lessons learned on the frontend development journey.

vue-transition-single-root.title: Vue <Transition> requires a single root node
vue-transition-single-root.summary: I spent an hour debugging a Vue warning until I understood why fragments break route transitions.
vue-transition-single-root.body:
Vue's <Transition> component cannot animate fragments — components with more than one root node in their template.

I was building my portfolio's HomeView with three root-level elements: a <CursorAura /> component, the main <section>, and a marquee <div>. Vue showed this in the console:

```
Component inside <Transition> renders non-element root node that cannot be animated.
```

**Why this happens**

Vue applies the `enter-active` and `leave-active` classes to the component's root DOM element. With a fragment there's no single element, so the transition silently fails.

**The fix**

Wrap the entire template in a single `<div>` container. It doesn't affect layout and resolves the warning entirely.

**What I learned**

Before debugging broken animations, always check that the component isn't a fragment. It's one of those Vue constraints that isn't clearly signaled in error messages.

css-background-clip-text.title: background-clip: text does not work through overflow: hidden
css-background-clip-text.summary: My gradient texts were invisible. The culprit was overflow: hidden on an intermediate container.

intersection-observer-pattern.title: IntersectionObserver: the correct pattern with Vue composables
intersection-observer-pattern.summary: I tried mounting the observer inside the composable but the ref was always null. The trick is to expose start() and let the caller control the timing.
